edges.quote: underestimated the will and determination of Alysanne Targaryen herself, and her young king, Jaehaerys.
edges.note: Council's secret betrothal of Alysanne to Orryn Baratheon drives Jaehaerys and Alysanne to flee to Dragonstone and marry in secret (seed row 312).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES/TRIGGERS: the betrothal is the provocation, but the flight and secret wedding are Jaehaerys and Alysanne's own free decision (agency-collapse check) -- target is the decision-event they produced.

edges.quote: That omission would prove to be of great importance when Lord Rogar and Queen Alyssa arrived belatedly from King's Landing in a war galley, accompanied by a dozen knights, forty men-at-arms, Septon Mattheus, and
edges.note: News of the unconsummated secret marriage draws Rogar and Alyssa to Dragonstone in force to try to undo it.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Rogar and Alyssa's voyage to intervene is their own decision made in response to the marriage, not a mechanical inevitability -- routes around agency-collapse.

edges.quote: The immediate cause of the tension was the king’s sudden and secret marriage to his sister
edges.note: Gyldayn names the secret marriage the 'immediate cause of the tension' between king and Hand that festers for a year until Rogar moves to depose Jaehaerys (seed row 93; grounding located in fab-surfeit-of-rulers-08-p01, the unit the S203 harvest row left null).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: many months and several free decisions separate the marriage from Rogar's coup attempt, but the text explicitly names the marriage as the origin of the estrangement -- a disposition edge to the decision-event where that estrangement erupts into action.

edges.quote: Alysanne must have her household, and in that her mother, Alyssa, saw an opportunity to undermine, and mayhaps undo, her marriage.
edges.note: Having failed to physically separate the couple at the gates, Alyssa (with Rogar's connivance) turns to a subtler plan: send companions to Dragonstone to undermine the marriage from within. [S204 adjudication: tier-2 — causation inferable-not-stated (failed gates confrontation -> household-undermine plan)]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: dispatching the Wise Women is Alyssa's deliberate follow-on scheme after the failed confrontation, a free decision, not an automatic consequence.

edges.quote: “We cannot remain here,” she confided to her dear companions.
edges.note: Lyman Lannister's growing ambition for a Targaryen alliance and dragons of his own drives Rhaena to resolve to leave Casterly Rock; after further wandering this resolve ends in her demanding, and receiving, Dragonstone as her own seat (seed row 97).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: several intervening stops (Ashemark, Castamere, the Golden Tooth, Wayfarer's Rest, Pinkmaiden) and a free grant decision by Jaehaerys separate Rhaena's resolve from the actual grant -- a disposition edge, not mechanical causation.

edges.quote: “Princess Aerea,” Lord Rogar answered at once. “There is a fire in her Jaehaerys does not have. She is young, but | can continue as her Hand, shape her, guide her, teach her all she must know. She has the stronger claim, her mother and father were King Aenys's first and secondborn, Jaehaerys was fourth.”
edges.note: Rogar's proposal to depose Jaehaerys and crown Aerea instead is met, in the same council session, by Alyssa's immediate dismissal of him as Hand (seed row 101).
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: same unbroken scene, nothing decisional between Rogar's proposal and Alyssa's response -- the very next beat.

edges.quote: One of those lost was Aerea Targaryen. On the night of Lord Rogar's dismissal, Ser Ronnal Baratheon and
edges.note: The text states explicitly that on the night of Rogar's dismissal, his brother Ronnal tried to seize Aerea from her chambers before the Baratheons quit the city.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: explicit same-night sequencing in the text ('On the night of Lord Rogar's dismissal').

edges.quote: only to find that Queen Alyssa had stolen a march on them. The girl was already gone, and her servants knew not where. It would be learned later that Lord Corbray had removed her, at the Queen Regent's command.
edges.note: Rogar's naming of Aerea as an alternative monarch in council put her in danger; Alyssa acted preemptively (via Lord Corbray) to spirit the girl away and hide her before the Baratheons could seize her.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Alyssa's protective action is her own decision, made in direct response to Rogar naming Aerea as claimant -- by the time Ronnal's men arrived, 'the girl was already gone.'

edges.quote: With Lord Rogar gone, Queen Alyssa named her brother Daemon Velaryon as Hand of the King, dispatched a raven to Dragonstone to tell her son Jaehaerys some (but not all) of what had occurred, and then retired to her chambers in Maegor's Holdfast.
edges.note: Immediately upon Rogar's departure, Alyssa names her brother Daemon Velaryon the new Hand.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'With Lord Rogar gone, Queen Alyssa named her brother...' -- the very next act in the same sentence, no intervening decision.

edges.quote: In war, he would boast, he would ne’er lay down his axe whilst life remained in his body...and this matter of the king’s marriage had become a war to him, one he was determined to win. One last folly remained to him, and he did not shrink from it.
edges.note: Humiliated by his dismissal and unable to accept defeat, Rogar attempts 'one last folly' -- sending his brother Orryn to seize the girl believed to be the true Aerea from the Oldtown motherhouse.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Rogar's decision to attempt the Oldtown ploy is a free (foolish) choice driven by the sting of his dismissal -- 'this matter of the king's marriage had become a war to him, one he was determined to win.'

edges.quote: On the twentieth day of the ninth moon of 50 AC, Jaehaerys Targaryen reached his sixteenth nameday and became a man grown. By the laws of the Seven Kingdoms, he was now old enough to rule in his own right, with no further need of a regent.
edges.note: The regency's legal end (Jaehaerys's sixteenth nameday) is the closing line of 'A Surfeit of Rulers'; 'A Time of Testing' opens immediately with the king's return to King's Landing to rule in his own right.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the majority is the section-break beat immediately followed by the king assuming personal rule -- same pattern as the Aegon III majority precedent named in the assignment notes.

edges.quote: The young king began his realm by remaking the council, keeping some men and replacing others who had proved unequal to their tasks.
edges.note: On his first day back, before anything else, Jaehaerys begins remaking the council.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: explicitly the king's first act upon return, no intervening decision.

edges.quote: All King’s Landing rejoiced when it was announced that Jaehaerys had also dismissed Edwell Celtigar as master of coin.
edges.note: Celtigar's failed, punishing taxes made him 'loathed throughout the city'; Jaehaerys's first act as king is to dismiss him as master of coin and strike down every one of his taxes.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Jaehaerys's choice to fire Celtigar is a free decision, driven by the taxes' visible failure -- the text plainly narrates the taxes' unpopularity immediately before narrating the dismissal that undoes them.

edges.quote: Only when all this had been done to his satisfaction and his new men were in place did Jaehaerys instruct
edges.note: Only once his own men are in place does Jaehaerys summon Rogar back to face him.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the text's own framing ('Only when all this had been done...did Jaehaerys instruct...') marks the council remaking as a satisfied precondition, not a forcing cause -- the summons and reconciliation remain Jaehaerys's free choice.

edges.quote: Across the width of Westeros, Rogar Baratheon fumed and raged when he learned of his brother's failure and imprisonment...but he did not call his banners, as many had feared. Instead he fell into despair.
edges.note: News of Orryn's failed ploy and imprisonment breaks Rogar's will to resist; he abandons any thought of calling his banners and instead awaits the king's judgment in despair.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the ploy's failure is the precondition that makes Rogar receptive when summoned to the throne room -- Jaehaerys's mercy remains his own free decision.

edges.quote: There, upon a canopied bed in Maegor's Holdfast in the Red Keep of King's Landing, the marriage of Jaehaerys Targaryen and his sister Alysanne was consummated at
edges.note: The public, consummated second wedding directly precedes Alysanne's pregnancy, the first fruit of the union.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: a natural, direct biological consequence of consummation, not mediated by a further free decision -- appropriate for the causal type rather than MOTIVATES.

edges.quote: With secrecy finally at an end, the king and his court waited to see how the realm would respond.
edges.note: With the marriage now public, Jaehaerys crafts (with Oswyck and, especially, Barth) the theological doctrine excusing Targaryen sibling marriage, and sends the Seven Speakers out to preach it.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the doctrine's creation is Jaehaerys's deliberate policy response to the marriage becoming known -- a free strategic choice, not an automatic effect.

edges.quote: We know the realm did not rise up against King Jaehaerys and Queen Alysanne in 51 AC as it had against Aegon and Rhaena ten years earlier. The why of it is a good deal less certain. The High Septon’s silence spoke loudly, no doubt, and the lords and common folk alike were weary
edges.note: Gyldayn credits the Doctrine of Exceptionalism and the Seven Speakers' preaching, alongside the High Septon's silence and the realm's war-weariness, as reasons no uprising greeted Jaehaerys and Alysanne's marriage as one had greeted Aegon and Rhaena's.
edges.rationale: CAUSES but tier-2/disputed: Gyldayn explicitly frames this as uncertain surmise among several possible causes ('The why of it is a good deal less certain... but... surely the Seven Speakers played a part as well'), not a flat assertion -- hedged historian's-judgment call.

edges.quote: Their uncle Maegor's taking of a second wife in 39 AC, in defiance of both the High Septon and his own brother, King Aenys, had shattered the delicate understanding between the Iron Throne and the Starry Sept, so the marriage of Aegon and Rhaena had been seen as a further outrage.
edges.note: Maegor's own second marriage in defiance of the High Septon (39 AC) is cited by Gyldayn as what had already broken the Iron Throne-Faith accord, making Aegon and Rhaena's later sibling marriage read as 'a further outrage' rather than an isolated affront.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plainly narrated cause-effect ('had shattered... so the marriage... had been seen as a further outrage'). Per the assignment note: verified this stub is the real 39 AC/Sons-of-the-Dragon event, referenced here only in retrospect -- wired honestly using the grounding text actually present in this assigned unit rather than left unwired or mis-flagged.

edges.quote: “So be it. Give me Dragonstone and one thing more, and | shall trouble you no further.”
edges.note: In the same negotiation, Rhaena parlays her new grant of Dragonstone into a second demand -- the return of her daughter Aerea -- which Jaehaerys grants in the same breath.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Rhaena explicitly bundles the Aerea request onto the Dragonstone grant ('Give me Dragonstone and one thing more') -- the first concession is the leverage/precondition for the second, which remains Jaehaerys's own free 'Done.'

edges.quote: at Bitterbridge, he passed Septon Barth coming in the other direction. Barth was alone, riding on a donkey.
edges.note: Septon Mattheus's dismissal and Septon Barth's summons to the royal library are both part of Jaehaerys's council remaking; their legendary crossing at Bitterbridge marks the changeover.
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF, not causal: this is an anecdote illustrating (not causing) the broader council overhaul -- structural containment is the honest type here, per the rule that a stub may take a role/structural edge when nothing causal fits.

edges.quote: His son Walton succeeded him, and when a sudden rebellion broke out in 50 AC amongst the men of the Night's Watch at Rimegate and Sable Hall, he gathered his strength and rode to the Wall to join the leal watchmen in putting them down.
edges.note: Brandon Stark's death puts his son Walton in the lordship, giving him the authority to ride to the Wall and put down the Rimegate/Sable Hall rebellion in which Olyver Bracken is beheaded.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Brandon's death is a precondition (it puts Walton in the seat that lets him respond) rather than a forcing cause of either the uprising or the beheading.

edges.quote: Once joined by Lord Stark and his bannermen, the black brothers retook Rimegate and hanged the oathbreakers, save for Ser Olyver himself, who was beheaded by Lord Stark with his celebrated blade Ice.
edges.note: Bracken's beheading is the climax of the Rimegate half of the suppression of the Night's Watch rebellion of 50 AC.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: structural containment -- an incident within the named rebellion, matching the existing wiki-sourced hub node (which already had zero causal edges).

edges.quote: Brandon Stark's second son, Alaric, became the Lord of Winterfell. He would rule the North for twentythree years, an able man though a stern one...but for a long while he had no good to say of King Jaehaerys, for he blamed the king's clemency for his brother Walton's death,
edges.note: Walton Stark's death in the rebellion's aftermath (set upon by giants while pursuing the Sable Hall rebels beyond the Wall) passes the lordship to his brother Alaric.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: succession follows automatically and immediately upon Walton's death during the rebellion, nothing decisional between the two beats.

edges.quote: When the queen’s party reached the docks, however, they found Ser Franklyn waiting for them. The rest of them could go, and good
edges.note: Franklyn's decree that Rhaena and her court may leave but Elissa must stay behind is what he tries to enforce at the docks the next morning, sparking the crowd's uprising against him.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the dockside confrontation is the direct, next-morning enforcement of the banishment terms, with nothing decisional intervening before the crowd's spontaneous reaction.

edges.quote: None of these measures had the desired effect of filling up the treasury vaults. Instead building slowed to a halt, the inns emptied, and trade declined notably as merchants diverted their ships from King’s Landing to Driftmark, Duskendale, Maidenpool, and other ports where they might evade taxation.
edges.note: Celtigar's failed taxes drove trade away and emptied the treasury; construction on the Dragonpit itself halted for lack of funds -- the fiscal emergency Rego Draz is brought in to resolve (seed row 266).
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Celtigar's tax failure creates the treasury emergency, but the choice to seek foreign loans (rather than, say, raise taxes further) is Rego Draz and Jaehaerys's free policy decision.

edges.quote: The immediate need for gold was resolved by Rego Draz, the new master of coin, who reached out to the lron Bank of Braavos and its rivals in Tyrosh and Myr to arrange not one but three substantial loans.
edges.note: Rego Draz's three loans from the Iron Bank and its Tyroshi and Myrish rivals immediately fund the resumption of work on the Dragonpit (seed row 266).
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the text states this plainly and directly ('The securing of the loans had one immediate effect; work on the Dragonpit was able to resume') -- explicit, undisputed cause-effect.

new_nodes.name: Betrothal of Alysanne to Orryn Baratheon
new_nodes.identity: The regency council -- Alyssa, Rogar, and their advisors -- settles swiftly on Orryn Baratheon, Lord Rogar's youngest brother, as husband for the thirteen-year-old Princess Alysanne, deciding the match without consulting her or the king; word of the plan reaches Alysanne before any formal announcement and spurs her and Jaehaerys to flee to Dragonstone and marry in secret instead.

new_nodes.name: Jaehaerys pardons Lord Rogar
new_nodes.identity: Summoned before the Iron Throne, Rogar Baratheon kneels and submits himself to the king's judgment; Jaehaerys pardons his 'foolish words' on the condition that Rogar champion Queen Alysanne and honor Queen Alyssa, remarries his mother and former Hand to each other, and sentences Orryn to ten years' exile rather than death -- closing the regency's final crisis without bloodshed.

new_nodes.name: Rego Draz secures three foreign loans
new_nodes.identity: Jaehaerys's new, foreign-born master of coin, the Pentoshi merchant Rego Draz, plays the Iron Bank of Braavos against its Tyroshi and Myrish rivals to arrange three substantial loans, easing the Crown's empty treasury and mounting debt.

new_nodes.name: Dragonpit construction resumes
new_nodes.identity: With the Crown's debt eased by Rego Draz's three foreign loans, builders and stonemasons once again swarm over the Hill of Rhaenys to resume work on the half-finished Dragonpit.

flags: Row 97 slug_candidates 'trystane-mounts-iron-throne' and 'judgment-of-princess-saera-before-the-iron-throne' are wrong (token-overlap noise) -- the real chain (Lyman Lannister's ambition for dragons/alliance -> Rhaena's resolve to leave the Rock) grounds in fab-surfeit-of-rulers-08-p02:73 and wires rhaenas-party-welcomed-at-lannisport -> grant-of-dragonstone-to-rhaena (C-E05).

Row 93's unit was null in the packet; grounding located at fab-surfeit-of-rulers-08-p01:35-37 ('The immediate cause of the tension was the king's sudden and secret marriage to his sister...'). Wired as C-E03.

maegor-takes-a-second-wife stub verified per assignment instruction: it is genuinely the 39 AC/Sons-of-the-Dragon event, NOT mis-titled -- fab-time-of-testing-09 only references it retrospectively (line 157) to explain why the Faith received Aegon and Rhaena's marriage so badly. Wired honestly (C-E19) using that retrospective grounding rather than left unwired.

restoration-of-aerea-to-rhaena's own text ('The consequences of this decision would not be known for years to come') foreshadows Aerea's later fate (existing nodes death-of-princess-aerea, balerion-bore-aerea-to-valyria) but that material lives in later F&B sections outside this assignment's units -- flagging for a future causal-spine session to wire restoration-of-aerea-to-rhaena forward to those nodes.

The Doctrine of Exceptionalism node (graph/nodes/concepts/doctrine-of-exceptionalism.node.md) currently carries only one Quotes entry from a much later section (fab-jaehaerys-and-alysanne-dragonstone-11); its founding scene is actually narrated in fab-time-of-testing-09:157-183 (Septon Alfyn's lion/aurochs sermon, Septon Baldrick's 'go to Dragonstone and claim a dragon' riposte) -- worth a quote-attachment pass, flagged in harvest below.

harvest.note: Rogar Baratheon: 'Blue-eyed and black-bearded and muscled like a bull... No swordsman, his lordship preferred to wield a double-bladed axe.'
Alysanne at 13: 'slim and slight of frame... eyes were blue rather than purple, her hair a mass of honey-colored curls.'

The apocryphal in-universe book 'A Caution for Young Girls' (also titled Sins of the Flesh / The High and the Low / A Wanton's Tale), purportedly by Coryanne Wylde -- a rich unreliable-narrator/in-world-textual-criticism episode not otherwise touched by this assignment's causal wiring.

Septon Alfyn's lion/aurochs sermon and Septon Baldrick's 'Go to Dragonstone and claim a dragon' riposte -- vivid Doctrine of Exceptionalism content not yet attached as Quotes to graph/nodes/concepts/doctrine-of-exceptionalism.node.md.

Jaehaerys's new spice/silk/gemstone/crenellation tax scheme, explicitly designed as voluntary luxury taxation ('a man need only forgo his pepper, his silk, his pearls') -- good material for a future economic-policy dip.

The 'Four-Headed Beast' court-within-a-court at Faircastle (Rhaena, Elissa Farman, Alayne Royce, Samantha Stokeworth) and Lannisport's lavish welcome feast with 'an entire stable... given over to Dreamfyre' -- hospitality/status material already partly on rhaenas-party-welcomed-at-lannisport but the Faircastle dynamic is not yet on any node.
